content.problemStatement:
Chef's professor gave him homework to find a permutation of length 2N2^N2N such that each integer from 000 to 2N−12^N  - 12N−1 appears **exactly once** in the permutation and the maximum bitwise XOR of any **even length** subarray is minimized.

Chef wonders how many such permutations are there. Help Chef to find out the number of permutations modulo (109+7)(10^9 + 7)(109+7), satisfying the above conditions and also help him find one such permutation.


Input Format

First-line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The only line of each test case contains an integer NNN.


Output Format

For each test case, print two lines of output.
In the first line, print the number of permutations modulo (109+7)(10^9+7)(109+7), for which maximum bitwise xor of any even-length subarray is minimized.
In the next line, print 2N2^N2N space-separated numbers denoting any such permutation.
constraints: Sum of 2N2^N2N over all test cases does not exceed 10610^6106

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: There are two optimal permutations: [0,1][0,1][0,1] and [1,0][1,0][1,0].
Test case 222: The maximum xor value of any even-sized subarray will be 222 and there are 888 such permutations.
